text.source_text: यदि तार्किक समय इव वेदान्तेष्वपि भिन्ना कारणावगतिरभविष्यत् , क्वचिच्चेतनं ब्रह्म जगतः कारणम् , क्वचिदचेतनं प्रधानम् , क्वचिदन्यदेवेति । ततः कदाचित्प्रधानकारणवादानुरोधेनापीक्षत्यादिश्रवणमकल्पयिष्यत । न त्वेत द स्ति । समानैव हि सर्वेषु वेदान्तेषु चेतनकारणावगतिः । ‘ यथाग्नेर्ज्वलतः सर्वा दिशो विस्फुलिङ्गा विप्रतिष्ठेरन्नेवमेवैतस्मादात्मनः सर्वे प्राणा यथायतनं विप्रतिष्ठन्ते प्राणेभ्यो देवा देवेभ्यो लोकाः ’ (कौ. उ. ३ । ३) इति , ‘ तस्माद्वा एतस्मादात्मन आकाशः सम्भूतः ’ (तै. उ. २ । १ । १) इति , ‘ आत्मत एवेदं सर्वम् ’ (छा. उ. ७ । २६ । १) इति , ‘ आत्मन एष प्राणो जायते ’ (प्र. उ. ३ । ३) इति च आत्मनः कारणत्वं दर्शयन्ति सर्वे वेदान्ताः । आत्मशब्दश्च चेतनवचन इत्यवोचाम । महच्च प्रामाण्यकारणमेतत् , यद्वेदान्तवाक्यानां चेतनकारणत्वे समानगतित्वम् , चक्षु रा दीनामिव रूपादिषु । अतो गतिसामान्यात्सर्वज्ञं ब्रह्म जगतः कारणम् ॥ १० ॥
text.transliteration_iast: yadi tārkika samaya iva vedānteṣvapi bhinnā kāraṇāvagatirabhaviṣyat , kvaciccetanaṃ brahma jagataḥ kāraṇam , kvacidacetanaṃ pradhānam , kvacidanyadeveti | tataḥ kadācitpradhānakāraṇavādānurodhenāpīkṣatyādiśravaṇamakalpayiṣyata | na tveta da sti | samānaiva hi sarveṣu vedānteṣu cetanakāraṇāvagatiḥ | ‘ yathāgnerjvalataḥ sarvā diśo visphuliṅgā vipratiṣṭherannevamevaitasmādātmanaḥ sarve prāṇā yathāyatanaṃ vipratiṣṭhante prāṇebhyo devā devebhyo lokāḥ ’ (kau. u. 3 | 3) iti , ‘ tasmādvā etasmādātmana ākāśaḥ sambhūtaḥ ’ (tai. u. 2 | 1 | 1) iti , ‘ ātmata evedaṃ sarvam ’ (chā. u. 7 | 26 | 1) iti , ‘ ātmana eṣa prāṇo jāyate ’ (pra. u. 3 | 3) iti ca ātmanaḥ kāraṇatvaṃ darśayanti sarve vedāntāḥ | ātmaśabdaśca cetanavacana ityavocāma | mahacca prāmāṇyakāraṇametat , yadvedāntavākyānāṃ cetanakāraṇatve samānagatitvam , cakṣu rā dīnāmiva rūpādiṣu | ato gatisāmānyātsarvajñaṃ brahma jagataḥ kāraṇam || 10 ||

translation.literal_translation: If, as in tarka systems, Vedanta too gave divergent causal knowledge - here conscious Brahman, elsewhere non-conscious Pradhana, elsewhere something else - then perhaps hearing of seeing/intending could be reinterpreted in line with Pradhana-cause doctrine. But this is not so. Across all Vedantas there is one and the same understanding of a conscious cause: from this self all pranas and worlds emerge; from this self space is born; from self alone this all is; from self this prana is born. We have already stated that atman-word denotes consciousness. And this uniform movement of Vedanta sentences toward conscious causality is a major ground of validity, like the eyes uniformly operating regarding forms. Therefore, by common drift (gati-samanya), the omniscient Brahman is cause of the world. (10)
translation.technical_translation: Bhashya on 1.1.10 invokes gati-samanya: cross-Upanishadic convergence on conscious atman-causality secures Brahman as jagat-karana against plural causal reinterpretations.
translation.interpretive_note: Corpus-wide semantic convergence is treated as an evidentiary principle.

lexical_notes.contextual_sense: Across Upanishads, discourse-direction converges on conscious atman-brahman causality.
Plural incompatible causes are denied by hermeneutic demand for coherent intention.
Bhashya refuses to treat Brahman, Pradhana, and others as parallel valid referents.
Repeated thematic vectors across passages are used as evidence, not mere style.
Convergent testimony fixes causality in conscious absolute rather than inert principle.

uncertainty_notes: Curated in contiguous S01 pass with stable inferential-tag schema.
A later philological pass should revisit svapya etymological nuances and gati-samanya phrasing in records 1.1.040-1.1.047.
